name: Italy
tagline: From the legend of Rome to a modern republic forged in the Risorgimento.
summary: Italy's history reaches from the Italic peoples, Etruscans and Greek colonists of the early peninsula, through the world-shaping Roman Republic and Empire, into a fragmented medieval and Renaissance era of papacy, communes, maritime republics and brilliant city-states. After centuries of foreign domination, the 19th-century Risorgimento welded these pieces into one state: the Kingdom of Italy was proclaimed on 17 March 1861, Rome was taken in 1870, and after the trauma of Fascism and the Second World War, Italians voted in 1946 to become a republic.

founding.label: Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy (Risorgimento)
founding.detail: Modern Italy dates its political founding to 17 March 1861, when the first Italian parliament in Turin proclaimed Victor Emmanuel II of Savoy King of Italy, the climax of the Risorgimento unification movement. Italians also remember the legendary foundation of Rome itself, traditionally dated to 753 BCE. Unification was completed only with the capture of Rome on 20 September 1870, after which Rome became the capital. Following the fall of Fascism and the Second World War, a referendum on 2 June 1946 abolished the monarchy and established the Italian Republic.

quickFacts.label: Republic established
quickFacts.value: Referendum of 2 June 1946; constitution in force 1 January 1948

quickFacts.label: European integration
quickFacts.value: Founding member of the EEC, Treaty of Rome 1957

quickFacts.label: UNESCO World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Holds more World Heritage Sites than any other country

eras.title: Italic Peoples and the Rise of Rome
eras.standfirst: Before Rome, the peninsula was a mosaic of Italic tribes, Etruscan cities and Greek colonies, out of which a small Latin town on the Tiber slowly grew.
body: Early Italy was a polyglot patchwork. Italic peoples such as the Latins, Samnites and Umbrians shared the peninsula with the Etruscans of central Italy, whose urban, artistic and religious culture deeply influenced their neighbours, and with Greek settlers who, from the 8th century BCE, founded the prosperous coastal cities of Magna Graecia in the south. Carthaginians and others contested the surrounding seas.

Greek colonization brought Hellenic civilization, alphabets, coinage and city planning to southern Italy and Sicily, leaving a lasting imprint that would later flow into Roman culture. The Etruscans, for their part, dominated much of the centre and north and according to tradition gave Rome several of its early kings.

Rome itself began, by legend, as a settlement on the Palatine Hill founded by Romulus in 753 BCE. Historians treat the regal period and its seven kings as largely legendary, but the tradition records a real transition: the expulsion of the last king and the establishment of the Roman Republic, conventionally dated to 509 BCE.

events.title: Legendary foundation of Rome
events.summary: Roman tradition, fixed by the scholar Varro, dates the founding of the city to 753 BCE, with Romulus as first king. The date is legendary, but it long anchored Rome's sense of its own origins.

events.title: Greek colonies of Magna Graecia
events.summary: From around the 8th century BCE Greek city-states founded colonies across southern Italy and Sicily, from Cumae to Tarentum, creating the prosperous Hellenic world of Magna Graecia.

events.title: Etruscan ascendancy in central Italy
events.summary: The Etruscans developed a sophisticated urban civilization in central Italy and, by tradition, supplied Rome with several of its kings before being gradually absorbed by the Roman state.

events.title: Founding of the Roman Republic
events.summary: Tradition holds that the Romans expelled their last king and established a republic governed by elected magistrates and the Senate, conventionally dated to 509 BCE.

figures.name: Romulus
figures.role: Legendary founder and first king of Rome
figures.blurb: In Roman myth Romulus, raised with his twin Remus, founded Rome on the Palatine in 753 BCE and became its first king. There is little historical evidence he existed, but the legend was central to Roman identity.

eras.title: The Roman Republic and Empire
eras.standfirst: From a city-state to a Mediterranean superpower, Rome built an empire whose law, language and institutions shaped Western civilization.
body: Over five centuries the Roman Republic expanded from central Italy to dominate the Mediterranean, defeating Carthage in the Punic Wars and absorbing the Greek east. Government rested on elected magistrates, the Senate and popular assemblies, but the strains of conquest, slavery and factional politics eventually broke the republican system in a century of civil wars.

After the assassination of Julius Caesar, his heir Octavian emerged victorious from the wars of the late Republic. In 27 BCE the Senate granted him the name Augustus, and he refounded the Roman state as the Principate while preserving republican appearances. As the first emperor he inaugurated the Pax Romana, roughly two centuries of relative internal peace and prosperity.

At its height the Empire stretched from Britain to the Near East, bound together by Roman law, roads, cities and Latin. Over the following centuries political instability, economic strain, and pressure on the frontiers weakened the western half of the empire.

By tradition the Western Roman Empire ended in 476 CE, when the Germanic commander Odoacer deposed the young emperor Romulus Augustulus and sent the imperial insignia to Constantinople. The eastern empire endured for nearly a thousand years more, but in Italy the classical order gave way to a fragmented medieval world.

events.title: The Punic Wars and Mediterranean supremacy
events.summary: In three wars against Carthage, Rome destroyed its great rival and became the dominant power of the western Mediterranean, beginning its rise to imperial dominion.

events.title: Augustus and the founding of the Principate
events.summary: The Senate granted Octavian the title Augustus, marking the start of the Roman Empire. As first emperor he established the Principate and ushered in the Pax Romana.

events.title: Completion of the Colosseum
events.summary: The Flavian Amphitheatre, the Colosseum, was inaugurated in Rome, a monument to the engineering and civic spectacle of the imperial city.

events.title: Edict of Milan and the rise of Christianity
events.summary: Constantine and Licinius granted toleration to Christianity, beginning its transformation from a persecuted faith into the dominant religion of the Roman world.

events.title: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
events.summary: The Germanic leader Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus, traditionally regarded as the last Western Roman emperor, ending Roman imperial rule in Italy.

figures.name: Augustus
figures.blurb: Born Octavian, he ended the civil wars of the late Republic and refounded the Roman state as the Principate, ruling as its first emperor and inaugurating the Pax Romana.

pullquote.text: He found a city of brick and left it a city of marble.
pullquote.attribution: Ancient saying about Augustus and Rome

eras.title: Medieval Italy: Papacy, Communes and Normans
eras.standfirst: Fragmented after Rome, Italy became a contested ground of popes and emperors, self-governing communes, and Norman kings in the south.
body: After the fall of the Western Empire, Italy passed through the rule of Ostrogoths, the Byzantine reconquest, and the Lombard invasions, leaving the peninsula politically divided. The Bishop of Rome, the pope, emerged as both a spiritual authority over Latin Christendom and a temporal ruler over the Papal States in central Italy.

From the 11th century, lacking a single dominant power, the cities of northern and central Italy increasingly governed themselves as communes. Their growing wealth and autonomy brought repeated conflict with the Holy Roman emperors, who claimed lordship over Italy, and divided Italians into Guelph and Ghibelline factions.

Along the coasts, the maritime republics, above all Venice and Genoa, but also Pisa and Amalfi, built commercial empires that linked Europe to the eastern Mediterranean and grew immensely rich on trade.

In the south, Norman adventurers conquered Sicily and southern Italy over the 11th century. In 1130 Roger II was crowned King of Sicily, uniting the region into a powerful, culturally mixed kingdom blending Norman, Greek, Arab and Latin traditions.

events.title: Lombard invasion of Italy
events.summary: The Lombards swept into Italy, fragmenting the peninsula into a patchwork of Lombard duchies and Byzantine territories and shaping its long political division.

events.title: Charlemagne crowned emperor in Rome
events.summary: Pope Leo III crowned the Frankish king Charlemagne emperor in Rome, binding Italy to the Frankish and later Holy Roman imperial order and to the papacy.

events.title: Roger II crowned King of Sicily
events.summary: Norman conquests in the south were united when Roger II was crowned King of Sicily, creating a wealthy multicultural kingdom of Latins, Greeks and Arabs.

events.title: Lombard League defeats Frederick Barbarossa at Legnano
events.summary: The allied northern communes defeated the Holy Roman Emperor Frederick Barbarossa at Legnano, securing recognition of communal liberties in northern Italy.

figures.name: Roger II of Sicily
figures.blurb: Norman ruler who united southern Italy and Sicily into the Kingdom of Sicily, presiding over an unusually cosmopolitan court that fused Latin, Greek and Arabic culture.

eras.title: The Renaissance and the City-States
eras.standfirst: In the wealthy city-states of Italy, a rebirth of classical learning and an explosion of art reshaped European culture.
body: From the 14th century, the prosperous and competitive city-states of Italy, Florence, Venice, Milan, Rome and others, became the cradle of the Renaissance. Drawing on the rediscovery of ancient Greek and Roman texts, humanist scholars placed renewed emphasis on human dignity, classical learning and the study of the world.

Florence, an independent republic, is often seen as the birthplace of the movement. Its wealth from banking and trade funded an extraordinary flowering of art and scholarship, much of it under the patronage of leading families, above all the Medici, who became the city's dominant power.

Renaissance Italy produced some of the most influential artists, architects and thinkers in history, while its diplomats and writers, including Machiavelli, helped found modern political thought. Italian art, architecture and ideas spread across Europe.

Yet political fragmentation left Italy vulnerable. From the late 15th century the peninsula became a battleground for the great powers of France and Spain in the Italian Wars, foreshadowing centuries of foreign domination even as its culture reached its height.

events.title: Competition for the Florence Baptistery doors
events.summary: The famous contest for the bronze doors of the Florence Baptistery, won by Lorenzo Ghiberti, is often taken as a symbolic opening of the artistic Renaissance.

events.title: Medici ascendancy in Florence
events.summary: Cosimo de' Medici returned to dominate Florentine politics, beginning the family's long patronage of art and learning that helped define the Renaissance.

events.title: French invasion and the Italian Wars
events.summary: The French invasion of 1494 opened decades of warfare in which France and Spain fought for control of Italy, exposing the weakness of its divided states.

events.title: Michelangelo paints the Sistine Chapel ceiling
events.summary: Commissioned by the papacy in Rome, Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling became one of the supreme achievements of High Renaissance art.

figures.name: Lorenzo de' Medici
figures.blurb: The effective ruler of Renaissance Florence, Lorenzo was a poet, diplomat and lavish patron whose support helped sustain the city's golden age of art and learning.

figures.name: Niccolo Machiavelli
figures.blurb: A Florentine diplomat whose treatise 'The Prince' analysed power with unsentimental realism, helping to found modern political philosophy.

pullquote.text: Florence, where the Italian Renaissance began, was an independent republic.
pullquote.attribution: On the cradle of the Renaissance

eras.title: Foreign Domination and the Risorgimento
eras.standfirst: After centuries divided under foreign crowns, a 19th-century movement for unity, the Risorgimento, forged a single Italian kingdom.
body: For much of the early modern period Italy remained politically fragmented and largely under foreign control, with Spanish and later Austrian Habsburg power dominant in much of the peninsula and the papacy ruling central Italy. Napoleon's conquests briefly reorganized Italy and spread revolutionary and national ideas before the old order was restored in 1815.

Through the 19th century the Risorgimento, the movement for Italian national revival, gathered force. Its leaders combined diplomacy and war: the statesman Camillo Cavour, prime minister of Piedmont-Sardinia, manoeuvred among the great powers, while Giuseppe Garibaldi led volunteers who, in 1860, toppled the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies in the south.

These efforts converged under the House of Savoy. On 17 March 1861, the first Italian parliament in Turin proclaimed Victor Emmanuel II King of Italy, uniting most of the peninsula into a single Kingdom of Italy and establishing the modern Italian state.

Unification remained incomplete until Venetia was acquired in 1866 and, after French troops withdrew, Italian forces breached the walls at Porta Pia and captured Rome on 20 September 1870. Following a plebiscite, Rome was made the capital, formally completing unification in 1871.

events.title: Napoleonic reorganization of Italy
events.summary: Napoleon's campaigns swept aside the old states and spread revolutionary, legal and national ideas across Italy, before the powers restored the former rulers in 1815.

events.title: Garibaldi's Expedition of the Thousand
events.summary: Giuseppe Garibaldi's volunteers conquered Sicily and southern Italy, bringing down the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies and clearing the way for unification.

events.title: Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy
events.summary: The first Italian parliament in Turin proclaimed Victor Emmanuel II King of Italy, the founding act of the unified Italian state and climax of the Risorgimento.

events.title: Capture of Rome
events.summary: Italian troops breached the Aurelian Walls at Porta Pia and took Rome from the Papal States; after a plebiscite, Rome became Italy's capital, completing unification.

figures.name: Giuseppe Garibaldi
figures.blurb: A charismatic military leader whose 1860 Expedition of the Thousand conquered the south and delivered it to the cause of a united Italy under the House of Savoy.

figures.name: Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour
figures.role: Architect of unification, Prime Minister of Piedmont-Sardinia
figures.blurb: The leading statesman of the Risorgimento, Cavour used diplomacy and war to expand Piedmont-Sardinia and engineer the unification of Italy under Victor Emmanuel II.

pullquote.text: On 17 March 1861 Victor Emmanuel II was proclaimed King of Italy.
pullquote.attribution: On the founding of the Italian kingdom

eras.title: Liberal Italy, Fascism, War and the Republic
eras.standfirst: A young kingdom passed through liberal politics and the First World War into Fascist dictatorship, catastrophic war, and finally a democratic republic.
body: The unified kingdom faced deep divisions between a wealthier north and poorer south, mass emigration, and the difficult task of building national institutions. Italy fought in the First World War on the Allied side from 1915, but the human cost and disappointment over the peace settlement left bitter discontent.

Amid postwar crisis and fear of socialist revolution, Benito Mussolini's National Fascist Party seized the initiative. After the March on Rome in October 1922, King Victor Emmanuel III appointed Mussolini prime minister, and over the following years Mussolini dismantled democracy and established a one-party Fascist dictatorship, the first of its kind in the world.

The regime's record includes the suppression of opposition, the 1938 racial laws against Italy's Jews, colonial aggression in Africa, and alliance with Nazi Germany. Italy entered the Second World War alongside Germany in June 1940. Military failure led to Mussolini's fall in July 1943 and an armistice with the Allies on 8 September 1943.

Italy was then torn between an Allied advance from the south and German occupation of the north, where Mussolini headed a puppet Italian Social Republic and an armed Resistance fought a brutal civil war. Mussolini was captured and executed by partisans on 28 April 1945. After liberation, a referendum on 2 June 1946 abolished the monarchy, and the republican constitution took effect on 1 January 1948.

events.title: March on Rome and Mussolini's rise to power
events.summary: After the Fascist March on Rome, King Victor Emmanuel III appointed Mussolini prime minister, beginning the transition to a Fascist dictatorship.

events.title: Fascist racial laws
events.summary: Mussolini's government enacted antisemitic racial laws that stripped Italian Jews of rights and citizenship, foreshadowing later deportations under German occupation.

events.title: Italy enters the Second World War
events.summary: Italy declared war on Britain and France, entering the Second World War as Germany's Axis ally, a decision that brought defeat, occupation and devastation.

events.title: Mussolini's fall and the armistice
events.summary: Mussolini was ousted in July 1943 and Italy signed an armistice with the Allies on 8 September; Germany then occupied the north, and a violent civil war and Resistance followed.

events.title: Birth of the Italian Republic
events.summary: In a referendum, Italians voted to abolish the monarchy and establish a republic; the new democratic constitution took effect on 1 January 1948.

figures.name: Benito Mussolini
figures.blurb: Founder of Italian Fascism who seized power in 1922 and ruled as dictator, abolishing democracy, enacting racial laws and allying with Nazi Germany. He was overthrown in 1943 and executed by partisans in 1945.

pullquote.text: The March on Rome marked the first fascist takeover of power in the world.
pullquote.attribution: On the rise of Italian Fascism

figures.name: Victor Emmanuel II
figures.role: First King of a united Italy (r. 1861–1878)
figures.blurb: King of Sardinia who became the symbol of the Risorgimento and, on 17 March 1861, the first king of a unified Italy, completing unification with the acquisition of Venetia and Rome.

figures.name: Leonardo da Vinci
figures.blurb: Painter of the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper and a tireless inventor and scientist, da Vinci embodies the Italian Renaissance ideal of the universal genius.
